summary:
Julius Caesar (100–44 BC) was the Roman general, statesman, and writer whose career effectively ended the Roman Republic and set in motion the transformation into the Roman Empire. A gifted military commander, political innovator, and supreme self-promoter, Caesar combined extraordinary abilities in nearly every field he touched: his Gallic Wars commentaries are still read as masterworks of Latin prose while documenting his conquest of Gaul (58–50 BC); his military tactics were studied for centuries; and his political maneuvers — the [[event:1073|First Triumvirate]] with Pompey and Crassus, his exploitation of the tribunicia sacrosanctity — showed complete mastery of Rome's constitutional machinery.

His crossing of the Rubicon in January 49 BC — leading his army into Italy in defiance of the Senate's orders — began a civil war that he won swiftly, defeating Pompey at Pharsalus (48 BC), campaigning in Egypt where he allied with [[event:1244|Cleopatra and Caesar]]'s Cleopatra, defeating Pharnaces II at Zela ('Veni, vidi, vici'), and crushing the last Republican forces in Africa and Spain. As dictator, he enacted sweeping reforms: the Julian calendar (replacing the chaotic Roman calendar), land distributions for veterans and the poor, the extension of citizenship, and ambitious building projects. His increasing autocratic style — accepting perpetual dictatorship, allowing divine honors, sitting while the Senate stood — provoked the conspiracy of senators led by Brutus and Cassius, who assassinated him on the Ides of March (March 15), 44 BC.

Caesar's assassination triggered the very civil wars his killers hoped to prevent: the [[event:1080|Battle of Actium]] power struggle eventually produced Caesar's adopted heir Octavian (Augustus) as sole ruler. Caesar's name became a title — Kaiser, Tsar, Shah, Czar all derive from it — and his legacy shaped governance and military strategy for millennia. His personal calendar reform, creating the 365.25-day year with a leap day, remained the basis of Western timekeeping until the Gregorian reform of 1582.
